opportunities.prompt: Illustration of a data center as a central cellular organ. A large glossy hub sits at the center representing the data center control, with three main streams radiating outward: Power depicted as a glowing lightning bolt, Data shown as flowing data packets, and Network as wavy signals. Surrounding racks resemble organs (liver-like servers, kidney-shaped cooling units). Use a clean vector style with cool blue/teal palette and lime highlights for data. Include labels Power, Data, Network and a small caption about enabling reliable computation for biology and education. Visual metaphor: living system that sustains computational biology workflows.
opportunities.purpose: Establish the living-system analogy and identify the data center core
opportunities.narrationContext: We introduce the data center as a central, living hub distributing energy, data, and signals to computational biology workflows.

opportunities.prompt: Two-panel infographic. Left panel shows the data center hub distributing energy, data, and signals to server racks; right panel shows computational biology workflows (sequence analysis, simulations, imaging pipelines) receiving resources. Use a consistent biology-inspired style, color-coded arrows, and labels mapping services to workflows. Minimal text for clarity.
opportunities.purpose: Bridge concept to real-world biology workflows and data needs
opportunities.narrationContext: Illustrate how core IT services enable biology research and education workflows.

opportunities.prompt: Infographic: Data center services mapped to cellular functions. Icons labeled Email, Data storage/management, Web hosting, Application hosting arranged as organs within a stylized cell. Central nucleus coordinates activities; arrows connect services to cellular roles such as communication, resource storage, and processing. Color palette aligned with slide 1 for consistency.
opportunities.purpose: Clarify the range of services and their relevance to lab data needs
opportunities.narrationContext: Explain each service in familiar life science terms and map to lab data needs.

opportunities.prompt: Close-up diagram of central management at the data center. The nucleus (central management) sends commands to servers, routers, and storage devices. Visuals include simplified icons for management, security, and operations with arrows showing control flow. Style: clean vector, consistent with slides 1–3, subtle lab-friendly background.
opportunities.purpose: Introduce centralized control and coordination of IT services
opportunities.narrationContext: Describe how centralized management coordinates multiple IT services, akin to how the nucleus directs cellular activity.

opportunities.prompt: Illustration of the data center physical backbone. Racks arranged as cellular organs; UPS depicted as a heart, PDU as vascular network; cooling units as lungs; raised floor visible with cabling like intracellular routing. Include labeled callouts for UPS, PDU, Servers, Routers, and Cooling Units. Style: vector with soft gradients; blue/green palette; readable captions.
opportunities.purpose: Visualize the physical backbone and critical components that keep systems powered and cooled
opportunities.narrationContext: Explain the core physical components that enable reliable operation and how power and cooling are distributed.

opportunities.prompt: Cross-section style diagram showing a raised floor as organized intracellular space. Cables flow under the floor like microtubules; airflow is depicted as blue arrows rising through racks; components include Servers, UPS, Cooling Units, PDU. Add labels and a small legend for airflow and cable management. Style: clean vector, lab-appropriate color coding.
opportunities.purpose: Show how physical layout supports air handling and cabling management
opportunities.narrationContext: Describe raised floors, airflow, and cabling as critical infrastructure for reliability.

opportunities.prompt: Side-by-side concept visuals: Economies of scale in data centers as tissue-level resource sharing. Left panel shows a large data center sharing resources across multiple labs; right panel shows a small operation duplicating efforts. Use color cues to emphasize cost savings, staffing efficiency, and shared resources. Caption: Economies of scale in education and science IT.
opportunities.purpose: Explain why larger facilities achieve reliability and cost benefits
opportunities.narrationContext: Relate large-scale data centers to tissue-level sharing and collective defense.

opportunities.prompt: Illustration of layered security as an immune defense: physical barriers (fence, badge access, surveillance) paired with virtual defenses (firewalls, antivirus). Central figure resembles a shielded organism defending a cell. Use dual-tone visuals, clear labels, and a cohesive color scheme with slide 1.
opportunities.purpose: Convey multi-layered security in data centers
opportunities.narrationContext: Describe how physical and cyber security work together to protect data and services.

opportunities.prompt: Tier levels visualization as a vertical stack of colored blocks labeled Tier 1 through Tier 4. Include icons representing redundancy, maintenance, and fault tolerance, plus short captions with availability numbers. Use a gradient from lower to higher reliability and annotate key features of each tier.
opportunities.purpose: Provide a clear, quick reference for Tier-related reliability and design characteristics
opportunities.narrationContext: Summarize what each Tier means in terms of structure and reliability.

opportunities.prompt: Availability vs downtime infographic by tier. Simple chart showing Tier 1 to Tier 4 with approximate downtime per year and matching cost implications. Include a small caption about $5,000 per minute outage and ~$300,000 per hour. Visual cues align with biology analogies (tissue stress and organism fitness).
opportunities.purpose: Translate reliability numbers into real-world decisions and budgets
opportunities.narrationContext: Explain how to map lab needs to an appropriate Tier based on acceptable downtime and cost.

opportunities.prompt: Supporting decision map for choosing Tier based on lab needs. Flowchart-style graphic showing questions like data criticality, acceptable downtime, budget, maintenance capacity, and recommended Tier ranges. Style consistent with previous slides, with biology-inspired icons.
opportunities.purpose: Provide a quick decision aid for educators and researchers
opportunities.narrationContext: Offer a practical takeaway to choose the appropriate tier for a given biology research scenario.

opportunities.prompt: Create a 20-second explainer video in a 2.5D vector style. The data center is portrayed as a living organism: nucleus governs services, mitochondria supply energy (power), bloodstream-like cabling carries data. Animate flow of power, data packets, and network signals through racks, with overlay text Power, Data, Network. End with a quick summary line emphasizing reliability enabling biology workflows.
opportunities.purpose: Demonstrate dynamic data flow and system coordination in action
opportunities.narrationContext: Show motion of energy, data, and control signals to reinforce how the center stays alive and responsive.

opportunities.prompt: Animation comparing Tier options with fault tolerance. 15-second clip showing four stacked blocks representing Tier 1–4, each block powering up with redundancy indicators. Include maintenance events and failover visuals, finish with a callout about the higher uptime of higher tiers.
opportunities.purpose: Visually compare robustness and maintenance implications across tiers
opportunities.narrationContext: Highlight how tier features translate to reliability and risk management.

opportunities.prompt: Cost of outages quick explainers: 15-second 2.5D video. A running clock shows minutes of downtime, with escalating cost per minute and related budget icons. End with a prompt showing that investing in higher reliability reduces risk and cost.
opportunities.purpose: Make outage cost tangible for biology educators and students
opportunities.narrationContext: Quantify the financial impact of downtime to inform tier decisions.

rationale: The media plan leverages a consistent life-science metaphor to translate abstract data-center concepts into familiar biology imagery. By placing a strong emphasis on the core flow of energy, data, and signals (Slide 1–3), then connecting to scale, security, and reliability (Slides 4–6), the visuals help a biology audience rapidly grasp infrastructure concepts and cost decisions. The mix of multiple images per slide (where helpful) and a few short videos at key moments ensures motion adds value for illustrating flow, redundancy, and the economic stakes without overwhelming the learner. The visuals are designed to be reusable across courses, lab talks, and student briefs, reinforcing core analogies across contexts.

estimatedCost.images: USD 540–1080 (assuming roughly USD 60–120 per image) for 9 images
estimatedCost.videos: USD 900–1800 (assuming USD 300–600 per video) for 3 short videos
